questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a Earth rotation model to check the key physics idea. What causes day and night on Earth?
options: The Sun moves around Earth once per day.
Earth moves closer to and further from the Sun each day.
The Moon blocks the Sun every night.
Earth rotates on its axis.
questions.explanation: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.
questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a day-night classroom globe to check the key physics idea. What causes day and night on Earth?
options: The Sun moves around Earth once per day.
Earth moves closer to and further from the Sun each day.
Earth rotates on its axis.
Earth stops spinning during the night.
questions.explanation: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.
questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a Sun-Earth diagram to check the key physics idea. What causes day and night on Earth?
options: The Sun moves around Earth once per day.
Earth rotates on its axis.
Earth moves closer to and further from the Sun each day.
Clouds cover the Sun on the night side.
questions.explanation: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.
questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

options: Earth rotates on its axis.

questions.explanation: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.
questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

questions.explanation: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.
questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a satellite orbit diagram to check the key physics idea. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth?
options: Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
There is no gravity in space, so the satellite floats in a straight line.
Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a Earth-orbit explanation to check the key physics idea. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth?
options: Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
Magnetism from Earth keeps every satellite moving.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a spacecraft path model to check the key physics idea. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth?
options: Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
The satellite is pulled outward by gravity.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a centripetal-force note to check the key physics idea. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth?
options: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
The Moon pushes the satellite around Earth.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a satellite orbit diagram to prepare for an exam-style question. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth? Choose the best answer.
options: Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
The satellite has no mass while in orbit.
Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.

questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a use of geostationary satellites
questions.question: A learner uses a communications satellite case to check the key physics idea. Which is a common use of geostationary satellites?
options: To measure the mass of atoms in the air.
To stop gravity acting on objects.
To make planets move faster.
Satellite television and communications
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.
questions.senNote: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a use of geostationary satellites

To stop gravity acting on objects.
Satellite television and communications
To remove the need for radio waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a use of geostationary satellites
questions.question: A learner uses a weather satellite example to link the context to the GCSE model. Which is a common use of geostationary satellites?
options: To measure the mass of atoms in the air.
Satellite television and communications
To make planets move faster.
To reflect all sunlight away from Earth.
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.
questions.senNote: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a use of geostationary satellites
questions.question: A learner uses a geostationary orbit note to link the context to the GCSE model. Which is a common use of geostationary satellites?
options: Satellite television and communications
To measure the mass of atoms in the air.
To make planets move faster.
To keep aircraft from needing engines.
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.
questions.senNote: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a solar system model to check the key physics idea. Which object is closest to the Sun?
options: Earth
Mars
Jupiter
Mercury
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.
questions.senNote: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a planet order revision card to check the key physics idea. Which object is closest to the Sun?
options: Earth
Mars
Mercury
Saturn
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.
questions.senNote: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a star colour chart to check the key physics idea. Which colour of star usually shows the highest surface temperature?
options: Red stars are usually the hottest.
Yellow stars always have the highest surface temperature.
All stars have the same surface temperature.
Blue
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a surface temperature comparison to check the key physics idea. Which colour of star usually shows the highest surface temperature?
options: Red stars are usually the hottest.
Yellow stars always have the highest surface temperature.
Blue
Blue stars are usually the coolest.
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a stellar classification note to check the key physics idea. Which colour of star usually shows the highest surface temperature?
options: Red stars are usually the hottest.
Blue
Yellow stars always have the highest surface temperature.
Star colour tells us only the star mass.
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a telescope observation table to check the key physics idea. Which colour of star usually shows the highest surface temperature?
options: Blue
Red stars are usually the hottest.
Yellow stars always have the highest surface temperature.
White stars must be colder than red stars.
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a star colour chart to prepare for an exam-style question. Which colour of star usually shows the highest surface temperature? Choose the best answer.
options: Red stars are usually the hottest.
Yellow stars always have the highest surface temperature.
Temperature cannot affect the light from a star.
Blue
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

Blue
A hotter star always looks darker.
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

Blue
questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.

options: Red stars are usually the hottest.

questions.explanation: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
questions.senNote: Blue stars are usually hotter than red stars.
